WMS-Rot Suite

WMS-FitRot

Rotational spectra overlay and targeted autofit companion for WMS-Rot.

A Note on Privacy

We strongly believe in collaboration and open science. Privacy and security are central to how this tool is designed. For us, the web is an open platform that, without relying on system-dependent compiled software, better reflects those values through accessibility, transparency, and portability.

Although WMS-FitRot is presented as a website, all code is executed locally on the user's own machine within the user's own web browser. No data is collected, uploaded, or processed by a remote background server, because there is no background server running the calculations. Everything runs locally in the browser. WMS-FitRot can also be saved as a web app on the computer, and it can continue to run even after the network connection is interrupted.

Uploaded Files

Upload experimental spectra or a Fit Status LOG and browse each file in its own tab.

How the derived stick spectrum is obtained from a continuous profile

In WMS-FitRot, a profile-mode .dat file is interpreted as an ordered sampled spectrum, i.e. a sequence of pairs (nu_i, I_i) sorted by frequency. The derived stick view is not a deconvolution or a full line-shape inversion; it is a peak-picked discrete surrogate of the sampled profile, intended for visualization, overlay, and assignment-oriented work.

Peak detection is performed on |I_i|, so that line finding is invariant with respect to whether the spectrum is later displayed in absorbance or transmittance convention. When no explicit threshold is supplied, the intensity floor is estimated robustly as median(|I|) + 5 * MAD(|I|), where MAD is the median absolute deviation.

A sampled point i is retained as a candidate transition only if it behaves as a proper local maximum: the left discrete slope must be positive, the right discrete slope must be non-positive, and the local second difference must be negative. In practice, this means that WMS-FitRot keeps only maxima that are both peak-like and above the robust detection floor; weak baseline structure and most noise excursions are therefore rejected automatically.

For every accepted maximum, the line center is refined by a three-point quadratic interpolation built from the neighboring samples (nu_(i-1), I_(i-1)), (nu_i, I_i), and (nu_(i+1), I_(i+1)). The reported stick frequency is the vertex position of that local parabola, and the reported stick intensity is the corresponding vertex height. If the interpolated vertex is numerically unstable, falls outside the bracketing interval, or yields a non-physical negative apex, WMS-FitRot falls back to the sampled maximum itself.

The resulting sticks should therefore be interpreted as experimentally detected peak positions and amplitudes extracted from the sampled profile. Closely blended features remain merged unless they generate distinct local maxima, and the stick intensities reflect the local peak envelope rather than fitted transition moments or a model-based unfolding of the instrumental profile.

No files loaded.

Overlay Spectra

Select the uploaded files and overlay profiles/sticks in absorbance or transmittance. All assignment clicks are performed on the overlay plot in this panel.

Upload at least one spectrum to enable the overlay.

Click on lines to select transitions to fit and make assignments!


Use these controls to decide how mouse clicks on the overlay spectrum are interpreted during assignment building.

Automatic mode

Choose where automatic clicks go
The selected box defines the effective click behavior on the overlay plot.

How automatic assignment uses fit and control labels

In automatic mode, clicks are accepted only on the selected theoretical trace in the overlay plot. Clicking a labeled theoretical line adds it to the currently selected target set; clicking the same label again removes it from that set.

Fit labels define the residual vector used in the least-squares refinement. Control labels are not fitted directly; instead, they are used after each local fit to rank candidate assignments through residual compactness, residual bias, and peak-collision consistency checks.

Supplying control labels explicitly is optional. If none are selected manually, WMS-FitRot can populate the control set automatically and use those lines only for post-fit scoring rather than for direct parameter optimization.

Manual mode
1
Click an orange theoretical line Choose the predicted transition.
2
Click an experimental line on another trace Select the observed counterpart on a different trace.
3
The accepted pair is stored as a 1:1 link The stored pair links the orange theory line to the selected experimental peak.

Manual mode expects one theory click first, followed by one experimental click.

Fitting Procedure

Click theoretical stick peaks to build the label list, then run the fit in-browser.

Inputs

This section defines the spectra and data mode used to build the fitting problem.

Represents the predicted labeled stick spectrum used as reference transitions. Changing it changes all target lines and the fitted model context. Represents the measured spectrum used as observation target. Changing it updates candidate peaks and residuals. Represents how experimental data are interpreted. Profile mode performs peak-picking first; stick mode uses provided lines directly. Available for stick spectra whose third column contains WMS-Rot labels. When enabled, WMS-FitRot reads those labels as explicit theory-to-experiment assignments and skips the assignment-search stage.

Selected labels

This section displays the fit labels, control labels, and manual pairs created from overlay-plot clicks and from manual text edits.

Use the click-behavior panel in Overlay Spectra to decide whether plot clicks create fit labels, control labels, or explicit manual pairs.

Fit labels Represents transitions directly used in least-squares residuals. Better-distributed, correctly assigned lines usually improve robustness.
Control labels Represents validation lines used to score assignment plausibility. They do not directly define fitted residuals but guide candidate selection.
Manual assignments (orange -> green) Represents explicit theory-to-experiment line pairs. Use this when you already trust assignments and want deterministic fitting.

Label input/Add/Clear let you edit selected transitions manually. Clearing labels resets fit/control/manual selections and restarts assignment setup.

Options

These groups control candidate generation, scoring, global search, and final numerical optimization.

Catalog And Bounds

Controls spectral catalog size and admissible ranges for rotational-constant updates during local/global fitting stages.

Span and Final span define the automatic relative bounds used in the local and final stages. The Custom Parameter Bounds section below is optional and only overrides the individual min/max sides that you fill in.

Represents the highest rotational quantum number used in theoretical simulation. Increasing it includes more transitions but raises computational cost. Represents the intensity threshold for keeping predicted lines. Lower thresholds include weak lines (more complete but noisier search space). Represents relative bounds for the initial local ABC fit. Larger span allows bigger corrections; smaller span enforces conservative updates. Represents relative bounds for the final global optimization stage. Wider bounds improve recovery from poor starts; tighter bounds stabilize refinement.
Assignment Search Not used in manual mode

Defines how candidate experimental peaks are generated and how broadly assignment combinations are explored before refinement.

Not used in manual mode. Explicit 1:1 theory-to-experiment pairs bypass combinatorial candidate search.

Represents the relative assignment window around each theoretical line (±Δ%). Increasing it captures more candidate peaks but increases ambiguity and runtime. Represents the tolerance used when declaring a control-line match (±Δ%). Increasing it is more permissive; decreasing it is stricter. Represents the maximum candidate peaks retained per fit label. Higher values improve coverage but expand combinatorics. Represents the maximum number of label-to-peak combinations evaluated. Increasing it improves search thoroughness but can be much slower. Represents stochastic sampling temperature for weighted combination generation. Higher values explore more diverse assignments; lower values favor near matches.
Additional Fitted Parameters

A, B, and C are always fitted. Select optional quartic or sextic distortion constants to include in the least-squares vector; unchecked parameters remain fixed at the current theory values. As an alternative to fitting quartic constants directly, you can fit a single quartic scale parameter s.

Represents which centrifugal distortion constants are promoted from fixed inputs to fitted variables. Leaving all unchecked preserves the current ABC-only behavior.
Applied quartic tau factor: 1/(s*s) = 1
Custom Parameter Bounds

Optional explicit overrides for the automatic span-based bounds. Leave a field empty to keep the bound generated from Span / Final span for that side.

Represents explicit lower and upper bounds passed to scipy.optimize.least_squares. This section does not replace Span / Final span globally; it only overrides the specific min/max sides you enter here.
Control Scoring Not used in manual mode

Defines how candidate assignments are ranked using control-line agreement, residual spread, bias, and peak-collision penalties.

Not used in manual mode. Manual assignments do not rely on control-line ranking or validation scoring.

Represents how many control lines are included in scoring when auto-control mode is used. More lines improve validation but increase evaluation work. Represents the penalty weight for control-line MAD (robust spread). Higher values prefer compact residual distributions. Represents the penalty weight for systematic residual bias. Increasing it discourages one-sided assignment errors. Represents the penalty weight for peak collisions (reusing the same peak). Higher values enforce cleaner one-to-one behavior.
Beam Refinement Not used in manual mode

Performs local neighborhood search around top-ranked combinations to recover better assignments missed in the first pass.

Not used in manual mode. Beam refinement applies only to the automatic assignment-search workflow.

Represents how many top combinations are kept as beam seeds. Larger beams increase recovery chance at higher runtime. Represents how many local alternatives per label are tested during beam expansion. Higher values broaden local search.
Local Refinement (Powell)

Powell performs derivative-free local search before final least-squares. It can improve convergence from rough initial constants, but it usually increases runtime.

Enable Powell local refinement before final least-squares. Disable it to run LS-only (faster, but sometimes less robust).
Least Squares (Final Stage)

Configures robust least-squares used in the final numerical refinement of A, B, and C after assignment selection.

Represents the robust loss model used by scipy.optimize.least_squares. Nonlinear losses reduce outlier influence. Represents how the robust scale parameter is set. Auto follows data-driven scaling; custom enforces a fixed sensitivity. Represents custom robust scale for residual weighting (used only in custom mode). Larger values make the fit less sensitive to moderate residuals. Represents the maximum number of function evaluations in final least-squares. Increasing it may improve convergence on hard cases.
Waiting for input…
Calculation may take a while, please do not close the window or considering running code locally
More Information on Sensitivity and Parameter Dependencies Open this section only when you want a deeper view of correlations, ranking, and fit stability.

Parameter Sensitivity

Local metrics derived from the Jacobian of the final fit.

Sensitivity Ranking

Parameter order by weighted sensitivity.

Parameter Correlations

Click a cell to highlight the selected parameter pair.

Selected Parameter

Fit Stability

Automatic Suggestions


      


      

        

          

            Fit Status LOG
          

          
Save the full WMS-FitRot session, including spectra, assignments, and fit parameter state, then reload it later.

          

            
          

        

        

          

            SPFIT Import
            

              ?
              

                
Import SPFIT Files

                

                  
Accepted files: session.json, .var, .fit, .out, .par, .txt.

                  
Parameter priority is .var > .fit > .out > .par > .txt. Include session.json when you want WMS-FitRot to restore exported assignments and session context.

                

              

            

          

          
Load a SPFIT seed or refinement package after reviewing the current fitting output.

          
          
        

      

    

  


  

    

    

      
File format

      
Does the file selected contain a sampled continuous profile or a stick spectrum?
